preFix.title: Five senses scan
preFix.subtitle: A three-minute meditation for arriving in the present moment through sensory attention
preFix.excerpt: A three-minute sensory scan that uses the five senses as anchors for present-moment attention. No history, no future. Just what is actually here right now.
preFix.sourceNotes: The five-senses grounding technique is widely used in mindfulness and therapeutic settings. Framing and script original to homemade.education.

content.text: A three-minute sensory scan. The five senses are the fastest route back to the present moment. They can only report on what is here now, not what was there then or what might happen next. Use this wherever you are.
Wherever you are, stop moving. If you can close your eyes, do. Take three slow breaths. Don't count them. Just let them arrive.
Move through each sense in turn, spending about twenty seconds with each:
**Sight.** Open your eyes softly. Name five things you can see right now, not categories but specific things. The chipped edge of the mug. The shadow on the wall.
**Sound.** What can you actually hear? Closest sounds first, then further away. Don't interpret the sounds. Just receive them.
**Touch.** What is the body in contact with right now? The pressure of the seat, the temperature of the air on the skin, the weight of the feet on the floor.
**Smell.** What is here? Don't invent. Just notice whatever is actually present, however faint.
**Taste.** What is in the mouth right now?
Take one more slow breath. You are here. That is the entire practice.
This technique is used in mindfulness and therapy. Script by homemade.education.

initialFindings.message: paragraph reads at grade 11.2 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
